title: Fixing CSS Variable Priority with Nested Selectors

statement.inputFormat: A string representing a CSS stylesheet.
statement.outputFormat: A string representing the corrected CSS stylesheet.
statement.notes: You can assume that the input string is a valid CSS stylesheet.

constraints.description: The number of selectors in the CSS stylesheet.
The number of CSS variables in the stylesheet.

examples.explanation: The variable `--primary-color` in the `.grandchild` selector should be `blue`, not `red`.
The variable `--background-color` in the `.grandchild` selector should be `#333`, not `#f2f2f2`.

hints.text: Consider using a stack data structure to keep track of the current selector scope.

editorial.title: Editorial: Fixing CSS Variable Priority with Nested Selectors
editorial.intuition: To fix the bug, we need to correctly calculate the priority of CSS variables when they appear in nested selectors. We can use a stack data structure to keep track of the current selector scope and update the variable priority accordingly.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible brute force approach is to parse the CSS stylesheet, identify all CSS variables, and then recursively traverse the selector hierarchy to update the variable priority.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use a stack data structure to keep track of the current selector scope and update the variable priority accordingly. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) where n is the number of selectors in the CSS stylesheet.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a stack data structure to keep track of the current selector scope and update the variable priority accordingly. This approach has a time complexity of O(n) where n is the number of selectors in the CSS stylesheet.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The proof of correctness for the optimal solution is based on the fact that we correctly update the variable priority for each selector and its nested selectors.
editorial.dryRun:
Here's a step-by-step example run of the optimal solution:
1. Initialize an empty stack to keep track of the current selector scope.
2. Parse the CSS stylesheet and identify all CSS variables.
3. Recursively traverse the selector hierarchy and update the variable priority accordingly.
4. Return the corrected CSS stylesheet.
commonMistakes: Failing to update the variable priority correctly for nested selectors
Using a brute force approach with a high time complexity
